title: Carney's First Year: How Canada's PM Turned Trump's Threats Into a Foreign Policy Reset
summary:
When Mark Carney took office as Canada's prime minister last year, he inherited a nightmare: a newly inaugurated U.S. president slapping tariffs on Canadian exports, threatening annexation, and upending a decades-long alliance. A year later, Carney is being applauded for turning that crisis into a strategic reset. "The most notable aspect of the last year was both a bullet dodged and a savvy bit of statecraft," said Brett House, a senior fellow at the University of Toronto's Munk School. Carney, he said, avoided the rush to cut a trade deal with the U.S. that many other nations pursued. "Commitments from this president are absolutely worthless."

Carney's approach has been to build at home and diversify abroad. With nearly 80% of Canadian exports historically going to the U.S., that dependence became a glaring vulnerability. He moved swiftly to thaw frozen relations with India, inviting Prime Minister Narendra Modi to the G7 summit in Canada to reset ties that had been in a deep freeze since 2023, when then-PM Justin Trudeau alleged Indian involvement in a killing on Canadian soil. Carney also recalibrated relations with China, strained since the 2018 arrest of Huawei executive Meng Wanzhou, and deepened partnerships with Japan, South Korea, Australia, and Europe. "He's doing this at a speed, scale and ambition that we haven't seen in recent years," said Vina Nadjibulla of the Asia Pacific Foundation of Canada.

Domestically, Carney has positioned himself as a centrist pragmatist, distancing himself from Trudeau's more progressive image. "He hasn't shown much interest in discussing things outside the economy, international relations and trade," said Sanjay Jeram, chair of political science at Simon Fraser University. "Canadians have bought that stance so far." Indeed, a March Ipsos poll pegged his approval at 58%, up 10 points from a year earlier. But his focus on economic efficiency has drawn criticism. A major projects bill designed to fast-track infrastructure has raised alarms that it could undermine consultations with Indigenous communities whose lands may be affected. As Carney enters his second year, the question is whether his pragmatic playbook can deliver on promises without leaving key voices behind.
why_it_matters: Carney's shift could permanently alter Canada's alliances and reduce its risk of being squeezed by a single powerful neighbor. At the same time, speeding up infrastructure without proper Indigenous consultation threatens to reignite domestic tensions over land rights and reconciliation.

keywords.explanation: Taxes that a government puts on imported goods, making them more expensive for domestic buyers.
The act of taking control of a territory by force or claim, making it part of one's own country.
The skillful management of a country's relations with other nations, using diplomacy and strategy.
A political position that avoids extremes and focuses on practical, realistic solutions rather than ideology.
A process designed to speed up the approval and construction of projects, often bypassing usual reviews.
The original peoples of a land whose descendants continue to maintain distinct cultural and legal identities.

options: Both India and China were specifically mentioned

options: It could undermine consultations with Indigenous communities

questions.question: Which senior fellow commented that 'Commitments from this president are absolutely worthless'?

background_read: Mark Carney (formerly the governor of the Bank of Canada and the Bank of England) became Canada's prime minister after a leadership race, stepping into a crisis with the newly inaugurated U.S. president. The president had imposed tariffs on Canadian steel and aluminum, threatened to withdraw from NAFTA, and even mused about annexing Canada as the '51st state'.

Canada has long been deeply integrated with the U.S. economy; roughly 80% of its exports go south. This dependence made the country vulnerable when the U.S. turned combative. Carney's strategy of trade diversification builds on decades of talk but little action, and his outreach to India and China marks a sharp pivot from the Trudeau years.

The freeze with India originated in 2023 when then-PM Justin Trudeau publicly alleged that Indian government agents were involved in the killing of a Sikh activist in British Columbia. Relations with China soured after the 2018 arrest of Huawei executive Meng Wanzhou. Carney's invitation to India's Prime Minister Narendra Modi to the G7 summit in Canada signals a willingness to reset ties despite past tensions.
Article_Structure: LEAD: A year after inheriting a nightmare with U.S. threats, Carney praised for strategic reset
└─ quote: 'both a bullet dodged and a savvy bit of statecraft'
KEY APPROACH: Build at home and diversify abroad
├─ nearly 80% of exports to U.S. was a vulnerability
├─ moved to thaw relations with India (inviting Modi to G7)
└─ recalibrated relations with China, deepened ties with Japan, South Korea, Australia, Europe
├─ distances himself from Trudeau's progressive image
├─ focuses on economy, trade, international relations
└─ raises alarms about undermining consultations with Indigenous communities
OPEN QUESTION: Can pragmatic playbook deliver without leaving key voices behind?

perspectives.description: Supporters argue that Carney's swift diversification and refusal to rush a trade deal protected Canadian sovereignty. His outreach to India and Europe, coupled with a centrist economic focus, has boosted national confidence and approval ratings after years of strain.

Critics warn that fast-tracking major projects could bypass meaningful consultations with Indigenous communities whose lands and rights are constitutionally protected. They fear economic efficiency is being prioritized over reconciliation and environmental safeguards.

Canada's historic overreliance on the U.S. market has been a structural weakness for decades. Carney's pivot mirrors a global trend of middle powers seeking to insulate themselves from great-power unpredictability, but such realignments take years and are vulnerable to electoral change.

Carney's second year will test whether his diversification can withstand a possible U.S. tariff escalation or a change in the presidency. Domestically, if the fast-tracking bill faces legal challenges from Indigenous nations, court rulings could redefine the balance between economic growth and Aboriginal rights.
